step.title: Step 86
step.actionDisplay: SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 10 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 182, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE toggles white strip length. Bar fills slowly with actions. Need to find faster way or confirm goal.
- Plan: Test if SPACE actions fill bar faster. Do batch of SPACE actions and measure bar change.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 181 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
10 SPACE actions reduced P from 9 to 6, so 3 pixels in 10 actions = 0.3 per action. This is the same rate as movement actions. So SPACE doesn't fill the bar faster.
The bar is at P=6 now. If the goal is to reach P=0, I need about 20 more actions. But I've already used 181 actions, which is way over the target of 71.
Let me reconsider the entire game. Maybe the goal isn't about the bar at all. Maybe the bar is just a timer/counter.
Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. Moving DOWN into the structures (not just adjacent to them)
2. Moving the player INSIDE a structure
3. Using the extended white strip to "paint" structures
4. Interacting with the blue (b) squares inside structures
5. Moving to the right structure (blue one)
Wait, I've been focusing on the left structure. What about the right structure? It's blue (b) with a gray (g) interior. Maybe I need to do something different with it.
Also, I notice the middle structure is c (charcoal) like the left one, and the right structure is b (blue). Maybe the goal is to make all structures the same color, or to change them in a specific pattern.
Let me try a completely different approach: move DOWN to enter the left structure (go inside it). The player is at rows 24-26, and the structure starts at row 28. There's a white strip at row 27. If I move DOWN, the player would go to rows 25-27 or 26-28, potentially entering the structure.
Actually, I tried DOWN before and the player didn't move. Let me check: the player is at rows 24-26. When I pressed DOWN earlier, the player stayed at rows 24-26. So DOWN doesn't move the player down when there's something below.
But wait, maybe the player CAN move down - I need to check if there's a gap. The white strip is at row 27, and the structure starts at row 28. So there IS a gap (row 27 is white, not a solid structure). Let me try moving DOWN multiple times to see if the player can enter the structure.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the white strip (W) is at rows 27-27, cols 16-19. The structure (c or G) is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. So the white strip is between the player and the structure. When I press DOWN, maybe the player moves onto the white strip, or maybe the white strip prevents movement.
Let me try pressing DOWN multiple times to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Bar fills at 0.3 px/action regardless of action type. P=6 now. Goal unclear - need to try entering structures.
Plan: Try moving DOWN multiple times to enter the left structure.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving DOWN multiple times
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN to enter structure')
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'G', 'c', 'b']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']} rows=[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}] cols=[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
N: pixels=12 rows=[24-26] cols=[16-19]
  W: pixels=4 rows=[27-27] cols=[16-19]
  G: pixels=12 rows=[28-31] cols=[16-19]
  c: pixels=12 rows=[28-31] cols=[24-27]
  b: pixels=28 rows=[28-31] cols=[28-39]
  b: pixels=4 rows=[29-30] cols=[17-18]
  b: pixels=4 rows=[29-30] cols=[25-26]
  c: pixels=12 rows=[32-35] cols=[16-19]
  b: pixels=4 rows=[33-34] cols=[17-18]
  c: pixels=59 rows=[63-63] cols=[5-63]
